behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is the real Git rename signal handoff into the CLI parser. This behavior depends on subprocess Git status/diff semantics; mocking Git output can hide real rename-detection edge cases (e.g., similarity thresholds, status codes). No LLM or multi-agent steps are involved.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temp repo, commit a file, rename it with content preserved, run the real CLI against the repo, and assert report shows 1 rename, 0 add, 0 delete for that change set.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output.
behaviors.justification: Real failure is usually encoding/subprocess-output handling with actual binary diffs. A mocked diff string may miss decode/pipe behavior and Git’s true binary markers, so mocks can falsely pass.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temp repo with a real binary file (e.g., random bytes), commit it, modify/replace it, run the CLI, assert process exits successfully and binary-related change is included in counts.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error.
behaviors.justification: This targets the real Git no-HEAD/no-commit state, which is an external subprocess/repo-state interaction. Mocking Git responses can hide actual error codes/messages and invocation behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a fresh repo with no commits, run the CLI, assert zero/empty structured report and successful (or expected non-error) exit status.

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on end-to-end interaction between repository history, Git query commands, parsing, and aggregation. Real subprocess + real repo history is the failure boundary; pure unit tests can miss command/format mismatches.
behaviors.test_description: Build a temp repo with a scripted sequence of commits containing known adds/deletes/renames/modifies, run CLI over the target range, and assert reported category totals match the known ground truth.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged.
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends on real Git index vs working-tree semantics and CLI invocation against those states. Mocking can hide real discrepancies in how commands read staged/unstaged changes.
behaviors.test_description: In a temp repo, create equivalent changes in two runs (one staged, one unstaged), execute CLI for each state, normalize deterministic fields, and assert reports are identical.
